summary: Stancil argues that if you want a Twitter profile picture that truly stands out, you should treat it as a color-contrast optimization problem rather than a flattering headshot. By sampling thousands of avatars and comparing dominant hues, he shows that extreme, edge-of-the-wheel colors maximize visual distinctiveness against both other profiles and Twitter’s UI.
money_quotes: In the middle of a sea of brown and beige heads, yellow stood out like a data scientist on a TV panel of seasoned political anchors.

Color science is complicated, and I don’t understand most of it. Figuring out which single color stands out the most in this rowdy mosaic requires a level of sophistication well beyond what my TV brain is capable of.
In other words, of all the colors on the visible spectrum, the best choice for my profile has to be among these eight.
While these colors are uncommon, they don’t necessarily stand out.
So green it is—scorching, unapologetic, and crackling new. Time to go get some likes.
key_insight: A purely attention-optimizing Twitter avatar is best achieved by choosing a highly contrasting, extreme color rather than a conventional photo.
